UX Case Study · Northeastern University · Spring 2026

PalUp

A mobile app that helps people find dining companions — reducing coordination friction and building trust between strangers, one meal at a time.

Karena, Zoey, Xinran, Suiyang
Chatroom · Research · Integration
Spring 2026

The Problem in a Sentence

"Finding dining companions is harder than it should be — people default to eating alone not because they want to, but because coordination feels too difficult."

8
Participants researched
4
Core task flows
5
Prototype iterations
4
Usability tests
01

The Problem

Context

Eating alone by default, not by choice

Many people — especially those new to a city or traveling solo — end up eating alone not because they prefer it, but because there's no easy way to find someone to share a meal with. The social infrastructure for casual dining companionship simply doesn't exist.

PalUp addresses this gap: a mobile app that lets users browse and create dining events, join small groups, and coordinate with other participants — all with a focus on reducing friction and building trust.

Research Question

What prevents people from dining with others?

We set out to answer: What barriers prevent people from finding dining companions, and what would make dining with strangers feel comfortable? Can regularly sharing meals help build social connections?

Target users: people newly arrived in a city, or solo travelers, whose social networks are still forming and who feel the barrier of eating alone most acutely.

02

User Research

Methods

Screening survey + semi-structured interviews

We used a two-phase approach: a screening survey to identify suitable participants, followed by semi-structured interviews to explore emotional and practical barriers in depth. Interviews were conducted via Zoom, WeChat, and in-person — 8 participants total.

Key Insight from Method

Behavioral questions over hypotheticals

Asking "would you dine with strangers?" produced overly positive answers. Switching to behavioral prompts — like "Describe the last time you changed plans because someone canceled" — revealed far more honest responses about discomfort, hesitation, and unmet social needs.

01
Trust & comfort are the real barriers

Not loneliness. The actual friction is not knowing if a stranger is safe or compatible. Participants wanted profile transparency before committing to a meal.

02
Practical constraints beat emotional needs

Scheduling conflicts, budget mismatches, and location inconvenience were cited more than loneliness. Design must reduce logistics friction first.

03
Small groups & restaurant type matter

Participants preferred groups of 2–4. Shared-style meals (hot pot, dim sum) were avoided when dining alone — the format demands company, not just preference.

→

Most people want dining companions but face friction in coordination and trust — not a lack of desire. The design opportunity is in making the process feel low-effort and safe.

03

Design Process

Paper Prototype

Paper Prototype

Create an Event

Paper Prototype

View Events & Chatroom

Paper Prototype

Profile & Settings

Phase 01

User Research

Screening surveys and semi-structured interviews with 5 participants. Used affinity diagramming to extract key themes around barriers, trust, and coordination.

Phase 02

Sketching & Paper Prototype

Each team member sketched their feature independently, then synthesized into a shared paper prototype covering all 4 task flows.

Phase 03

Heuristic Evaluation

Applied Nielsen's 10 heuristics to identify usability issues. Documented redesign decisions before moving to Figma wireframes.

Phase 04

Usability Testing

Each team member tested their task flow with one external user. Findings were incorporated into the final high-fidelity prototype.

04

Design Iterations

Each change was driven by usability testing or heuristic evaluation findings. Below are the most significant design decisions across the project.

Task Flow Issue Found Design Change
Create Event Home screen doubled as event creation — users couldn't tell if they were browsing or creating. Violated visibility of system status. Heuristic Fix
Added a floating "+" button in the bottom-right corner. Clear separation of browsing and creating.
Create Event Users found the "Vibe" field label unclear. Options (Quiet/Casual/Deep) were too abstract to interpret confidently. User Testing
Renamed to "Dining Atmosphere" with clearer options: Quiet Meal / Casual Hangout / Deep Conversation.
Join Event No confirmation step — users could accidentally join an event. Violated error prevention. Heuristic Fix
Added a confirmation popup ("Confirm join this event?") before finalizing the join action.
Join Event Participant avatars had no interaction. Users expected to see who they'd be dining with before joining. User Testing
Made avatars clickable to show participant profile — name, gender, bio — supporting trust-building.
Chatroom Toggling "Mute Notifications" produced no visible feedback. Users were unsure if the action worked. User Testing
Replaced with "Pin Notice to the Top" — important info stays pinned in the main chat view for all participants.
Login & Profile No dedicated login page. No confirmation after saving profile changes. Heuristic Fix
Added a proper login screen with error states. Added save confirmation after profile edits.
05

Final Prototype

Overview

The final prototype covers four core task flows: logging in and managing a profile, browsing and joining dining events, creating and publishing an event, and communicating with participants in a group chatroom. The design prioritizes trust-building, low-friction coordination, and clear feedback at every step.

Key Features

Home feed with event cards showing restaurant, location, group size, and budget. Floating "+" button for quick event creation. Clickable participant avatars for profile previews. Group chatroom with pinned notices. Report functionality for safety. Confirmation dialogs to prevent accidental actions.

Home Feed
Event Detail & Join
User Profile
Chatroom with Pinned Notice
06

Discussion

What We Learned from Usability Testing

Across four usability test sessions, a consistent theme emerged: users struggled most with feedback visibility and discoverability. When actions produced no visible response — such as the mute notification toggle or the missing error state on login — users became uncertain whether the system had registered their input at all. This points to a broader gap in system transparency throughout the prototype.


Tasks that were well-supported by clear visual hierarchy — such as browsing events on the home feed — were completed quickly and with confidence. Tasks requiring users to discover hidden interactions, such as tapping participant avatars or navigating to chatroom details, caused friction and hesitation.

Key Design Recommendations

1. Pinned notice over mute toggle. Users could not tell whether muting notifications had any effect. We replaced this with a visible pinned notice at the top of the chatroom — directly addressing the need for important information to be seen without requiring users to take extra steps.


2. Tappable event cards on home feed. After creating an event, users expected to tap the new card to view its details — but nothing happened. Making all event cards tappable removed this confusion and aligned with users' mental models.


3. Clearer label language. "Vibe" was replaced with "Dining Atmosphere" and abstract options were rewritten as descriptive phrases. This reduced cognitive load and helped users make confident selections.


4. Clickable participant avatars. Users wanted to know who they would be dining with before joining. Making avatars clickable to reveal profiles directly addressed the trust barrier identified in our original research.

07

Conclusions

Future Work & Limitations

Our usability tests were limited to one participant per task flow, which constrains the generalizability of our findings. Future iterations should include broader recruitment — particularly users outside our personal networks — to reduce self-report bias and capture more diverse dining behaviors. A key area for future development is a smarter matching system based on conversational compatibility, which our research identified as more important than food preferences alone.

Next Steps

The final prototype builds directly on the high-fidelity Figma prototype and incorporates all changes identified through usability testing and heuristic evaluation — including the pinned notice feature, tappable event cards, clearer field labels, and clickable participant avatars. These updates bring the prototype closer to a product that genuinely reduces friction and builds trust for people seeking dining companions.
